region_of_origin: China (Shanghai / Jiangnan region); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Steamed filled buns (baozi) with pork filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03302050) but no specific entry for pork-filled steamed bun (baozi). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EKX covers steamed bread/bun as a category. No specific pork-filled baozi code exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for steamed pork bun as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One steamed pork bun (baozi) weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at a dim sum restaurant or as a single-piece takeaway item.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed pork bun (baozi) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite reference; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for steamed filled buns
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exactly matches a restaurant-style steamed pork bun. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entry for 'Pork bun, steamed' (NDB No. 18447 / FDC ID 172030 region) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo entries for steamed filled buns. Per-100g macros are consistent with a bun composed of approximately 55% wheat-flour dough and 45% seasoned pork filling. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a standard steamed pork bun of this type.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of steamed wheat-flour bun with pork filling — analogous estimate based on steamed Chinese bun (baozi) GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for a restaurant-style steamed pork bun (xian rou bao). GI estimated at 45 based on steamed Chinese wheat-flour buns (baozi/mantou variants) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Steamed wheat-flour buns with meat filling have a lower GI than baked bread due to the steaming process and the presence of protein and fat from the pork filling, which slow gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 25.2 g available carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 11.3.

hypertension.guidance: A single steamed pork bun (80 g) provides approximately 304 mg sodium, representing about 13% of the 2,300 mg daily limit; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one piece and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as soy sauce dips.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat carbohydrate content and glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low-to-moderate GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of about 11 per piece, a single steamed pork bun is manageable within a diabetes meal plan, but portion control is essential as multiple pieces will cumulatively raise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each steamed pork bun contributes approximately 1.8 g saturated fat and 304 mg sodium per piece; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one piece per meal and pair with low-sodium, vegetable-rich dishes to maintain a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A steamed pork bun (鲜肉包, xiān ròu bāo) is a classic Chinese dim sum and everyday snack consisting of a soft, fluffy, leavened wheat-flour dough wrapper encasing a savoury minced or chopped pork filling seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, spring onion, and a small amount of sugar. The bun is cooked entirely by steaming, which gives the exterior a smooth, slightly glossy, pillowy texture and keeps the interior moist. The filling is juicy and umami-rich, with a mild sweetness from the dough contrasting the savoury pork. Originating in the Jiangnan region of China and popularised across Chinese-speaking communities worldwide, the steamed pork bun is a staple of dim sum restaurants, hawker centres, and takeaway food stalls throughout Singapore. A typical serving is one piece weighing approximately 80 g, providing around 178 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and 25.2 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), making it more glycaemically moderate than baked bread due to the steaming process and the protein-fat matrix of the filling. Clinically, the primary concerns are moderate sodium content (approximately 304 mg per piece) and refined carbohydrate load, which are relevant for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The bun contains gluten and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the pork filling.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a steamed pork bun?
How much sodium in a pork bao?
Steamed pork bun GI glycemic index
Din Tai Fung steamed pork bun calories

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source provides exact per-100g nutrient data for a restaurant-style steamed pork bun (xian rou bao). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed bun and pork filling references, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for steamed filled buns. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed Chinese wheat-flour buns. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo source data for this food type. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source references used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100g based on trace levels typical of pork fat; this is a low-confidence estimate. The food is not halal-certified as it contains pork. The Din Tai Fung restaurant context from the input record is preserved as provenance context but nutrient values are not sourced from restaurant data. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific product has not been identified in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the processing date.
